questions: Where is the megaplan source code? The sandbox restricted access to the package installation. Is it an editable install from a local repo, or installed from a registry? The exact file paths in the plan are educated guesses that Step 1 will resolve.
Does the finalize phase already run any project tests (e.g., as part of preflight checks)? The SKILL.md mentions 'preflight_results' in the gate phase but it's unclear if tests are part of that.
Is there a standard way to detect or configure the project's test command, or would this be new infrastructure?
Should the baseline capture be opt-out (enabled by default) or opt-in? Enabling by default prevents scope creep immediately but adds latency to finalize for projects with slow test suites.

success_criteria.criterion: Finalize phase captures a test baseline artifact (JSON) containing pre-existing test pass/fail status before execution begins
Execution briefing includes a clearly labeled section listing pre-existing test failures when baseline has failures
Executor does not attempt to fix pre-existing test failures that are listed in the baseline
Review phase evaluates test success criteria as 'no regressions from baseline' rather than 'all tests pass'
Baseline capture gracefully handles: no test command found, test runner errors, timeouts — finalize still completes
All existing megaplan tests pass after changes
Baseline capture completes within configurable timeout (default 5 minutes)
Baseline feature is configurable (can be disabled, custom test command, custom timeout)
Baseline JSON artifact is human-readable and includes test names, files, and failure status
If pre-existing failures are incidentally fixed during execution, this is reported as a bonus in review, not as required work

assumptions: The megaplan source is a standard Python package installed in the pyenv environment, with a command-based module structure (e.g., megaplan/commands/finalize.py). Step 1 will verify this.
The finalize phase produces a briefing document (likely markdown) that is consumed by the executor agent. The baseline section will be added to this existing document.
The executor is an LLM agent that follows instructions in the briefing — so 'updating the executor' means updating prompt text, not runtime code logic.
The review phase has access to plan artifacts and can load the baseline JSON to compare against post-execution test results.
Pre-existing test failures are the primary source of executor scope creep. Other sources (e.g., linter warnings, type errors) are out of scope for this change.
Baseline capture should be enabled by default (opt-out) since the whole point is to prevent scope creep proactively.
Test detection can use simple heuristics (presence of pytest.ini, package.json with test script, Cargo.toml, etc.) — a perfect solution is not needed for v1.
structure_warnings: Each step section should reference at least one file in backticks.
